Yes, it is possible to run a different `create_app` function or pass parameters to it when running a CLI command, without relying on environment variables. One approach is to use a custom CLI command that can detect whether it's being run in a server context or a CLI context. This detection can be done by checking if the CLI command is being executed as part of a Flask application. 

In the Flask app factory pattern, the `create_app` function is typically called once when the app is initialized. However, when running CLI commands, the Flask CLI is already initialized, and you can access the app object before it's fully created. 

This means that you can check if the CLI command is being run in the context of the Flask app. If it is, you can then use a different `create_app` function or pass specific parameters to it. 

By using a custom CLI command, you can bypass the need for environment variables. This makes your CLI commands more user-friendly and easier to use. 

You can define a custom CLI command that checks the context in which it's running. If it's running as part of the Flask app, it can then call `create_app` with specific parameters. 

This approach allows you to have a single `create_app` function that can be configured differently depending on how the app is being used. 

For example, when running the app as a server, you can use the default `create_app` function. But when running a CLI command, you can call a modified version of `create_app` with additional parameters. 

This method also allows you to pass parameters to `create_app` when running the CLI command, without needing to set environment variables. 

By using a custom CLI command, you can avoid the need for external variables and make your CLI commands more streamlined. 

You can also use the `flask` command-line interface to run your CLI commands directly, which makes it easier to manage your application's behavior. 

Another benefit of this approach is that it keeps your configuration and CLI commands more centralized, reducing the risk of errors. 

You can use the `@app.cli.command` decorator to define CLI commands that can be run using the `flask` command. 

When defining these commands, you can pass parameters to them, which can then be used to modify the behavior of the app. 

You can also use the `flask` command to run your CLI commands, which makes it easier to integrate them into your development workflow.
